title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Scientific Question Answering
text:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Scientific Question Answering Authors: A. Rivera, J. Okafor, M. Lindqvist (synthetic example paper) Abstract
We study retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for answering questions over a
corpus of scientific articles. Standard large language models hallucinate
citations and conflate findings across papers. We propose SciRAG, a pipeline
that grounds every generated sentence in retrieved passages and attaches
inline citation markers. On a benchmark of 1,200 expert-written questions,
SciRAG reduces unsupported claims by 63% relative to a non-retrieval baseline
while improving answer correctness.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Scientific Question Answering
text:
of 1,200 expert-written questions,
SciRAG reduces unsupported claims by 63% relative to a non-retrieval baseline
while improving answer correctness. Methods
SciRAG splits each paper into overlapping passages of roughly 1,000 characters
and embeds them with a contrastively trained dense encoder. At query time we
retrieve the top-k passages by cosine similarity from a FAISS index and prompt
the generator to cite passage identifiers. A lightweight verifier re-checks
each sentence against its cited passage and flags sentences whose claims are
not entailed by the citation. Flagged sentences trigger a second retrieval
round with a query expanded by the verifier. Results
SciRAG attains 81.4% answer correctness versus 68.0% for the closed-book
baseline and 74.2% for a single-pass RAG system without verification. The
verifier's re-retrieval step contributes a further 4.1 points. Human raters
judged 92% of SciRAG citations to be relevant, compared with 61% for the
single-pass system. Latency increases by 1.7x due to the verification round.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Scientific Question Answering
text:
92% of SciRAG citations to be relevant, compared with 61% for the
single-pass system. Latency increases by 1.7x due to the verification round. Limitations
The verifier itself can miss subtle unsupported inferences, and the approach
assumes the answer is contained in the corpus. SciRAG was evaluated only on
English-language biomedical and machine-learning papers; generalisation to
other domains and languages is untested. The dense encoder was not stress
tested on out-of-distribution terminology, and no ablation isolates the effect
of chunk size on retrieval quality.

title: Multi-Agent Reflection Improves Long-Form Reasoning
text:
Multi-Agent Reflection Improves Long-Form Reasoning Authors: P. Mensah, L. Conti, R. Bhattacharya (synthetic example paper) Abstract
We investigate whether decomposing a reasoning task across cooperating agents
improves the quality of long-form answers. Our framework, ReflectGraph, runs a
proposer agent that drafts an answer, a critic agent that evaluates grounding
and completeness, and a controller that decides whether to revise. Across
three reasoning benchmarks, ReflectGraph improves factual accuracy by 11 points
and reduces logical inconsistencies by 38% compared with single-pass
generation. Methods
ReflectGraph is implemented as a directed graph of agents with a bounded
revision loop. The proposer produces a structured answer with explicit claims.
The critic scores each claim for support and coverage and, when coverage is
low, emits a refined sub-question. The controller terminates when the critic
reports sufficiency or a maximum of three revision cycles is reached. All
agents share a common evidence pool so revisions accumulate context rather than
discarding it.

title: Multi-Agent Reflection Improves Long-Form Reasoning
text:
or a maximum of three revision cycles is reached. All
agents share a common evidence pool so revisions accumulate context rather than
discarding it. Results
On scientific synthesis tasks, ReflectGraph reaches 0.86 coverage as judged by
experts, versus 0.71 for a single proposer. The bounded loop converges in 1.8
revisions on average. Disabling the critic removes most of the gain, confirming
that explicit evaluation, not merely additional generation, drives the
improvement. The controller's stopping rule avoids the degenerate behaviour of
endlessly rewriting confident-but-wrong answers. Limitations
The critic can be over-confident and occasionally halts before coverage is
truly adequate. Costs scale with the number of revision cycles, and the method
assumes the proposer and critic do not share the same systematic blind spots.
Experiments did not measure robustness to adversarial or contradictory evidence
in the shared pool, and human evaluation was limited to a single annotator pool.

title: Dense Retrieval with Contrastive Pretraining for Literature Search
text:
Dense Retrieval with Contrastive Pretraining for Literature Search Authors: H. Nakamura, S. Adeyemi, T. Varga (synthetic example paper) Abstract
Effective retrieval is the foundation of any grounded question-answering
system. We present ConText, a dense passage retriever pretrained with a
contrastive objective over 8 million scientific abstracts. ConText improves
top-5 retrieval recall on a literature-search benchmark from 0.62 to 0.79 over
a sparse BM25 baseline, and transfers to unseen subfields with minimal
degradation. Methods
We mine positive passage pairs from citation links and section co-occurrence,
and sample hard negatives using an in-batch strategy refreshed every epoch.
Passages are encoded into 384-dimensional embeddings that are L2-normalised so
that inner-product search recovers cosine similarity. We index embeddings with
a flat inner-product structure for exact search and evaluate approximate
variants for scale. Retrieval quality is measured with recall@k and mean
reciprocal rank.

title: Dense Retrieval with Contrastive Pretraining for Literature Search
text:
structure for exact search and evaluate approximate
variants for scale. Retrieval quality is measured with recall@k and mean
reciprocal rank. Results
ConText raises recall@5 to 0.79 and MRR to 0.61, outperforming BM25 (0.62 /
0.44) and a general-purpose dense encoder (0.71 / 0.52). Hard-negative
refreshing accounts for roughly half of the improvement over the general
encoder. On three held-out subfields, recall drops by only 3 points on average,
indicating reasonable domain transfer. Exact inner-product search remains fast
enough for interactive use at the evaluated corpus size. Limitations
Contrastive pretraining is computationally expensive and depends on the quality
of mined citation links, which are noisy in emerging fields. ConText was not
evaluated on full-text retrieval, only abstracts, and the approximate index
variants trade a small amount of recall for speed in ways that were not
characterised for very large corpora. No experiment measured calibration of the
similarity scores themselves.
